content:
Applause is cheap. Integration risk is not.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The demo was perfect. Six minutes. Agent read a ticket, opened the repo, wrote a migration, added tests, opened a PR. The screen shared well. Leadership clapped in the way people clap when they think they have seen the future arrive early.
Then the meeting ended and the real work started.
Not the agent’s work. Ours. The quiet work nobody schedules because it does not fit in a keynote: who owns the service, which environment gets the credentials, what the rollback looks like when the migration runs against production data instead of demo seed data, whether legal has an opinion on the log line the agent added that includes email addresses.
Applause is cheap. Integration risk is not.
What the Demo Optimized For
Demos optimize for legibility in a conference room. Production optimizes for surviving contact with customers, finance, compliance, and the other team whose queue your webhook now blocks.
The gap shows up immediately after applause:
→ Demo used a repo with no legacy constraints → Demo credentials had admin scope nobody gets in prod → Demo tests mocked the payment vendor; prod vendor rate-limits and retries → Demo PR merged to a branch; prod needs staged rollout, feature flags, and on-call sign-off → Demo agent had no pager history for this service; your team does
I sat in the follow-up with platform, security, and the squad that actually maintains billing. The champion who ran the demo was excited. Platform was polite. Security asked one question about secret storage and got a vague answer. Billing said nothing for four minutes, which is never good.
The question that broke the mood was mine, and I regret that I waited until minute twelve: “Who is on call for this after we merge?”
Silence. Not dramatic silence. Worse. The kind where everyone realizes the demo shipped a narrative, not a system.
The Integration Checklist Nobody Wants to Read
We built a checklist after that meeting because shame is a underrated project management tool. Not a gate for experimentation. A gate for production promotion. Demos can skip it. Customer-facing merges cannot.
Our post-demo checklist includes:
→ Service owner named in catalog with current pager rotation → ADR or equivalent for persistence, retries, and external calls → Secrets path approved by security, not copied from demo .env → Staging load test on the dependency that demo mocked → Rollback tested, not theorized → Observability: logs, metrics, alerts tied to business outcomes → Runbook entry shorter than one page, written for the paged person at 3am → Legal/compliance review if PII, payments, or retention changed
The agent can help with several items. It cannot sign the owner field. Ownership is a human assignment backed by employment contract and on-call stipend.
Billing’s first real contribution was small and devastating: the agent’s webhook handler was not idempotent. Demo retries did not exist. Production retries would duplicate invoices. Fixable. Would have been a headline if we merged on demo momentum.
Security’s second contribution hurt ego: the demo stored API keys in environment variables named like production but scoped like god mode. Least privilege meant rewriting configuration, not tweaking a line. Two days of work hidden inside a six-minute miracle.
Warm honesty: the demo was still valuable. It proved the workflow could produce a plausible starting point. Starting points are not shipments.
Why Teams Rush After Applause
Momentum is dangerous after a good demo. Leadership wants a win on the roadmap. Champions want budget. Vendors want a case study. Everyone has incentive to treat integration work as “cleanup” instead of the main event.
I have watched three agent rollouts in two years. The ones that survived had the same pattern: a boring week after the demo where velocity looked flat. The ones that failed skipped that week and celebrated merged lines instead.
Flat velocity after applause usually means:
→ Someone is wiring auth correctly → Someone is fixing the migration for real row counts → Someone is arguing with security about least privilege → Someone is writing the alert that fires when queue depth spikes → Someone is teaching support what the new failure mode looks like
None of that fits in six minutes on stage. All of it determines whether customers notice.
If your organization punishes the flat week, you will get demo-grade code in production and demo-grade incidents in status pages.
How to Run the Second Meeting Well
Schedule it before the demo if you can. If you cannot, hold it within forty-eight hours while memory is fresh and before narrative hardens into budget.
Invite the people who were not in the room:
→ On-call primary for affected services → Security delegate who can say no without career risk → Representative from downstream consumers → Finance or ops if money movement touched → Support lead if user-visible behavior changed
Agenda in plain language:
→ What did the demo prove? → What did it explicitly not prove? → What must be true before production promotion? → Who owns each blocker? → What date is realistic if we do not skip steps?
The champion presents the artifact. Platform presents constraints. Security presents questions, not lectures. Billing presents failure modes. Support presents language customers will use when it breaks.
End with owners and dates, not applause.
Our second meeting took seventy minutes. We left with fourteen action items. Only four involved the agent. The rest involved adults assigning responsibility. Two weeks later we shipped to ten percent of traffic. No heroics. No rollback. The demo started the story. The second meeting made it true.
Support added one item I still quote: user-facing error strings. The demo used developer jargon in toast messages. Support would have eaten that alive on Monday. Copy changed before staging, not after Twitter noticed.
What I Tell Teams Now
When someone asks me to demo agents for executives, I say yes — and I ask for the second meeting on the calendar before the first one happens. Executives who care about outcomes understand. Executives who only care about optics sometimes push back. That pushback is data about whether your organization is buying speed or buying theater.
Engineers early in career should watch both meetings. The first shows tooling potential. The second shows seniority: naming risk, assigning ownership, refusing to merge without rollback, translating vendor retry semantics into idempotency keys.
Demos are recruitment tools for belief. Integration is recruitment tools for trust. Customers do not clap. They expect invoices to be correct.
A week after our second meeting, the champion asked if we could demo again for the board. I suggested we demo the rollback instead. Show the runbook. Show the alert that fires when queue depth crosses threshold. Show the idempotency test that failed first and passed after integration work. Less flashy. More board-worthy if the board actually owns risk.
They demoed the rollback. Questions were better. Budget for staging environments appeared in Q3 planning without a fight. Turns out executives understand “we can undo this in four minutes” more than “the agent wrote a migration.”
Junior engineers on the team started volunteering for integration checklist items instead of racing to the next demo. That shift mattered more than any plugin update. Ownership is a career skill demos do not teach.
If you are building a career around agents, optimize for the second meeting skills. Prompt craft matters. Catalog ownership matters more. Knowing which questions make security relax matters more than any plugin.
The warm part: second meetings are where teams learn to trust each other. Demos are performance. Integration is collaboration. I would rather hire someone who runs a boring seventy-minute blocker session than someone who only ships keynote diffs.
Applause fades before the pager fires. Integration risk is what you are paid to carry.
When you want the production patterns, failure modes, and system design judgment that belong in the second meeting — not the demo script — The Production Engineering Library: The Complete Collection for Production Systems, System Design & Backend Engineering is the stack I open before anyone says “ship it to prod.”
